turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the court this morning in case 22-7466, Glossip v. Oklahoma.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: This case has a long history. Nearly three decades ago, Justin Sneed murdered Barry Van Treese, the owner of a Best Budget Inn in Tulsa, Oklahoma. At the same time, Sneed was doing housekeeping tasks for Glossip, the inn's manager. After killing Van Treese, Sneed confessed his crime to Glossip, who feigned ignorance for a day before telling the police. When police apprehended Sneed, he initially told them that Van Treese died in a robbery gone wrong. Later, Sneed said that he had committed the murder at Glossip's instruction. Oklahoma charged both Sneed and Glossip with murder. In return for agreeing to testify against Glossip, Sneed pled guilty and avoided the death penalty. At trial, Sneed's testimony was the only direct evidence linking Glossip to the murder. After a first trial verdict was overturned on appeal, Oklahoma tried Glossip again, and a jury convicted him of murder and sentenced him to death. Glossip continued to maintain his innocence. Eventually, both the Oklahoma Legislature and the Oklahoma Attorney General grew concerns about the integrity of Glossip's conviction. Both hired independent investigators to look into Glossip's case. Each investigation uncovered a host of prosecutorial misconduct, ranging from the destruction of evidence to attempts at influencing Sneed's testimony. Eventually, the State disclosed new evidence to Glossip, including a note from the lead trial prosecutor showing she knew at trial that Sneed lied on the stand about his psychiatric testimony, yet failed to correct his false testimony. After that discovery, Glossip filed a habeas petition in Oklahoma State Court to challenge his conviction. The attorney general joined Glossip's request for a new trial, taking the highly unusual step of confessing error. Specifically, Attorney General Drummond conceded that the prosecution, knowing failure to correct Sneed's false testimony on the stand, had violated this court's decision in Napue v. Illinois. Napue held that the due process clause requires prosecutors to correct false testimony when it occurs at trial. The Oklahoma Court of Criminal Appeals deny Glossip's unopposed request for relief. Its decision is difficult to parse, but it says among other things that an attorney general's confession of error can overcome other state law limitations on post-conviction relief, only if the confession has a basis in the law and the facts of the case. In Glossip's case, the court would not accept the confession of error because it did not think the prosecution had violated its obligation to correct false testimony. As a result, the court held that a State law limitation on post-conviction release barred Glossip's claims. The court faces two questions, whether it has jurisdiction over Glossip's claims and whether the prosecution violated its obligation to correct false testimony. Begin with jurisdiction. This court cannot review a State court decision if that decision rests entirely on an adequate State law rule. After all,State courts are the ultimate authority on State law. Sometimes, however,State law rules themselves depend on federal law. For example, a State law rule might apply only to unmeritorious federal claims. In those cases, the real question is one of federal law, and that means this court has jurisdiction. That is the situation here. The Oklahoma courts have held for more than a century that if an attorney general confesses error in a criminal case, they will reverse the conviction unless the conviction lacks a basis in law and fact. In this case, the attorney general confessed to an error of federal law, namely a violation of this court's decision in Napue. The Oklahoma court rejected the confession and declined to reverse the conviction because it explained it did not believe the prosecution had violated the federal due process clause. Oklahoma's rejection of the attorney general's confession thus rested on its evaluation of federal law. That means this court has jurisdiction to review the judgment. Turning to the substance of Glossip's claim, the court holds that the prosecution violated its duty to correct false testimony. To win on a claim like this, a petitioner has to point to false testimony at his trial and show that the prosecution knew it to be false and failed to correct it anyway. As the State of Oklahoma admits, the record in this case shows all those elements. At trial, Sneed testify that someone had given him lithium after he asked for cold medication and that he had never seen a psychiatrist. The record shows the prosecution must have known, that in fact, a jail psychiatrist had given Sneed lithium to treat bipolar disorder, yet the prosecution never corrected Sneed's misstatement. Under this court's clear precedence, its failure to do so violated the due process clause. A prosecutor's failure to correct false testimony is often a material error requiring a trial, and that is true in this case too. Sneed, recall, was the only witness tying Glossip to the murder for which the jury sentenced him to death. Had the jury known Sneed was unwilling to lie at trial about one thing, it may have been more skeptical about the rest of his story as well. Moreover, Sneed's previously untreated psychiatric condition, combined with his known abuse of acid, meth, and cocaine, could have helped support the defense theory that Sneed committed the murder on his own as an impulsive act of violence, as Sneed first confessed to having done. So, the prosecution's failure to correct Sneed's lie could have affected the jury's judgment in Glossip's case, and that is all Glossip must show to prevail. For the reasons just explained and others said out in the opinion, the court now reverses the judgment below and holds that Glossip is entitled to a new trial. Justice Barrett filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part. Justice Thomas filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Alito and Justice Barrett joined in part. Justice Barrett took no part in the consideration of this case.
